images.alt: A black-background fabric with loads of weird sketchy U-shaped designs of varying sizes and orientations, filled in with various colours and textures and designs (it's a "modern art" print), and rectangles. Colours include lots of pale creams (usually used to outline the "U"s), navy, beige, gold, coral pink, purple, sky blue, royal blue, crimson, ruby red, lime green, lemon yellow, orange, and more.

A diagonally and vertically repeating Frank Lloyd Wright design of overlapping and intersecting circles, arcs, and rectangles like a modern stained glass window.

There are big circles composed of lots of shades of green with arcs of grey-pink, dark green, and blue running through them, and overlapped with clusters of smaller circles like bunches of grapes in predominantly shades of blue. The rectangles fill in the negative space in shades of pale yellow, pale blue and deep green.

images.alt: Gallus. A Glasgow SF Writer's Circle Anthology. Front cover. A woman with red hair, pink skin. Dressed in reds and greens, blues and stars. Gold bells as earrings. A fish in her shoulder, another by her hand, which is wearing a gold ring. A sea bird flying in from the side. There are bubbles in the air. Edited by E. M. Faulds, Brian M. Milton & Neil Williamson.

The back cover of the book, continuing the themes of the front, colours and fish and stars. There is a strip showing landmarks of Glasgow, particularly those around the SECC where Worldcon will take place. A man on a horse rides by, a traffic cone on his head. The text overlay defines "gallus" - a Scottish adjective "bold, daring, high spirited." The spirit of Glasgow, the well that has fuelled the pens of the GSFWC for almost 40 years. This anthology is a snapshot of the circle today, 25 fantastical stories, spanning a spectrum of genres and interests from Scottish myths to the far reaches of the galaxy.

Background, blurred image taken from the cover in the other images. Gallus - a Glasgow SF Writer's Circle anthology. Releasing August 2024, join us for the launch at Worldcon in Glasgow. Featuring: C. J. Henderson, Hal Duncan, Phil Raines & Harvey Welles, Annabel Campbell, Laura Elise Jenkins, Cameron Johnston, Heather Valentine, Ian Hunter, PS Livingstone, Robin CM Duncan, T. H. Dray, Richard Mosses, Ruth EJ Booth, Alan M Laird, E. M. Faulds, Brian M. Milton, Stewart Horn, Sophie C. Baumert, Elsie WK Donald, Don Redwood, Neil Williamson, Jenni Coutts, Ewan Lawson, M. Nesce Drake and me!

images.alt:
We got home yesterday from White Rock BC from spreading my mother in law’s ashes with her two grandchildren.. 

So I enjoyed some “fiction” and read this book as we went through the dead zones in the mountains. (I’m a Virgin user for city’s but they are awful rural.. whatever I’m an urban Native, lol!)

I loved the short stories in Midnight Storm Moonless Sky. It talked about everyday racism smoothly with everyday spirituality. Yeah some were pretty spooky but mostly tragic or sad like the majority of ghost stories are rooted with. Idk if I will ever be able to read non Indigenous authors much anymore but I loved every word on these pages. Still thinking of some of the stories what the follow up might be.. the best fun of reading. So pick this book up if you get a chance!

images.alt: Stunning lily mostly in the shade with a highlight on a couple petals from the sunshine peeking through. Its colour is a beautiful soft salmon with yellow in the centre and a lighter pale salmon stripe down the centre of each petal. The stamen are yellow and casting soft shadows on the petal below them. Dark green leaves and black shadow behind the flower, with a second lily of the same variety in the left corner in the near distance.

A bunch of Coneflowers or Echinacea, beautiful gradient hot pink to salmon to orange tip petals and dark reddish brown textured bulbous centres. There are white coneflowers and magenta ones behind those, out of focus in the distance.
A large magenta coneflower in the foreground with a large fuzzy bumblebee on the textured reddish centre. Other magenta coneflowers in the background and greenery.
I forgot what these pretty purple small five petal flowers are called but there’s a cluster of three against bright green leaves in the shade.
